fever.title: High Fever / Hyperthermia
fever.summary: A high fever indicates your body is fighting infection. Act quickly — above 40°C becomes life-threatening.
steps: Move to a cool, shaded area and rest immediately
Remove excess clothing and heavy blankets
Drink clean water or oral rehydration solution every 15 minutes
Apply a cool (not cold) damp cloth to forehead, neck, armpits, and groin
Fan the person continuously to increase evaporative cooling
Take paracetamol/ibuprofen if available — follow dosage on packaging
Monitor temperature every 30 minutes if possible
Keep the person awake and talking if temperature exceeds 39°C
If no thermometer: check for hot, dry skin, rapid pulse, confusion — these indicate dangerous fever
avoid: Do NOT use ice-cold water — it causes shivering and raises internal temperature
Do NOT give aspirin to children under 16
Do NOT bundle the person in heavy blankets
Do NOT leave the person alone if they are confused or unresponsive
Do NOT give food until fever breaks — fluids only
when_to_seek_help: Temperature above 40°C (104°F) that won't come down
Person is confused, having seizures, or unconscious
Fever in infants under 3 months old
Fever lasting more than 3 days
Severe headache, stiff neck, or skin rash appears
Person stops producing urine — severe dehydration

bleeding.title: Severe Bleeding / Hemorrhage Control
bleeding.summary: Uncontrolled bleeding kills in minutes. Stop the bleed first — everything else waits.
steps: Put on gloves or use a clean plastic bag to protect yourself from bloodborne disease
Apply firm, direct pressure on the wound with the cleanest cloth available
Do NOT remove the cloth — if it soaks through, add more material on top and press harder
Elevate the injured limb above heart level if no fracture is suspected
For limb wounds with severe bleeding: apply tourniquet 5–7cm above the wound, tighten until bleeding stops
Write the exact time of tourniquet application on the person's forehead or skin with marker/blood
Pack deep wounds tightly with cloth — push material deep into the cavity with your fingers
Keep the person warm, flat, and legs elevated to prevent shock
Talk to them calmly — consciousness is a vital sign, keep them responding
Improvised tourniquet: use a belt, cloth strip, or clothing — twist with a stick until bleeding stops
avoid: Do NOT remove an embedded object — stabilize it in place with padding around it
Do NOT remove a tourniquet once applied — only a medic should do this
Do NOT give water if the person may need surgery soon
Do NOT leave the person alone
Do NOT use a thin cord or wire as a tourniquet — it causes tissue damage without stopping bleeding
when_to_seek_help: Bleeding does not slow after 10 minutes of direct pressure
The wound is deep, wide, or has exposed bone or tissue
Blood is spurting rhythmically — this is arterial bleeding, immediately life-threatening
Person becomes pale, cold, confused, or loses consciousness
Tourniquet has been on for more than 2 hours
Signs of infection: red streaks spreading from wound, increasing pain, foul smell

cpr.title: CPR — Cardiac Arrest / Not Breathing
cpr.summary: Brain death begins in 4–6 minutes without oxygen. Start CPR immediately — imperfect CPR is infinitely better than none.
steps: Check scene safety — do NOT become a second victim
Tap shoulders firmly and shout 'Are you okay?' — confirm unresponsiveness
Shout for help — send a specific person to call emergency services
Tilt head back, lift chin — open the airway
Look, listen, feel for breathing for no more than 10 seconds
If not breathing normally: place heel of one hand on center of chest between nipples
Place other hand on top, interlace fingers, keep arms straight
Push hard and fast — at least 5cm (2 inches) deep at 100–120 compressions per minute (think: Stayin' Alive rhythm)
Allow full chest recoil between compressions — don't lean on chest
After 30 compressions: tilt head, lift chin, pinch nose, give 2 rescue breaths (1 second each, watch chest rise)
If untrained or unwilling to give breaths: do continuous chest compressions only — still effective
Continue until emergency services arrive, person starts breathing, or you are physically unable to continue
If AED available: turn it on immediately and follow its voice instructions
avoid: Do NOT stop CPR to check for pulse unless the person clearly moves or breathes normally
Do NOT compress too slowly — you need 100–120 per minute minimum
Do NOT compress too shallow — must be at least 5cm deep on adults
Do NOT tilt head if you suspect spinal injury — use jaw thrust instead
Do NOT give up early — people have survived after 45 minutes of CPR
when_to_seek_help: Any cardiac arrest — call emergency services immediately and simultaneously
After successful resuscitation — person needs hospital evaluation
If person doesn't respond to CPR after 30 minutes with no return of circulation

fire.title: Fire / Immediate Evacuation
fire.summary: You have 2–3 minutes before fire makes a building uninhabitable. Escape first, everything else is replaceable.
steps: Alert everyone nearby immediately — shout 'FIRE!' continuously
Activate the nearest fire alarm if available
Before opening any door: feel it with the back of your hand — if hot, do NOT open
If door is cool: open it slowly, stay low, look for smoke before proceeding
Stay low to the ground — clean air is below 60cm from the floor
Use stairs only — never elevators in a fire
If trapped in a room: seal door gaps with clothing or towels, signal from window
Signal from window with a bright cloth, phone light, or shout — firefighters look for movement
If clothes catch fire: STOP (don't run), DROP (fall to ground), ROLL (until fire extinguished)
Once outside: move at least 100 meters away, account for everyone, do NOT go back
If escaping through smoke: hold damp cloth over mouth and nose
avoid: Do NOT try to collect belongings — not worth your life
Do NOT open hot doors
Do NOT use elevators
Do NOT go back into a burning building for any reason
Do NOT stand upright in heavy smoke
when_to_seek_help: As soon as you are safe — call emergency services
If anyone is injured, burned, or missing
If there is a gas leak involved
If the fire is near fuel, chemicals, or vehicles

electricity.title: Electrical Emergency / Power Outage
electricity.summary: Electricity kills silently and instantly. Disconnect the source before you touch anyone.
steps: If someone is being electrocuted — DO NOT touch them while still in contact with source
Turn off the main power switch/breaker immediately if safely accessible
Use a dry wooden stick, rubber item, rope, or plastic to push the source away from the person
Once disconnected from source: check breathing — start CPR immediately if not breathing
Treat electrical burns: cool with clean water for 20 minutes, do not use ice
For power outage: unplug sensitive electronics to protect from power surge when electricity returns
Use battery-powered or hand-crank lights — avoid candles near flammable materials
Keep refrigerator closed — food stays safe 4 hours; freezer 48 hours if full
If using a generator: run it OUTSIDE minimum 6 meters from windows/doors — carbon monoxide is odorless and fatal
avoid: Do NOT touch an electrocuted person while they are still in contact with the source
Do NOT use metal objects to separate a person from electrical source
Do NOT run generators, gas stoves, or grills indoors — CO kills in minutes
Do NOT assume a downed power line is safe — stay 10 meters away minimum
Do NOT step in water near electrical equipment
when_to_seek_help: Anyone has been electrocuted — internal injuries may not be visible
Sparking, burning smell, or smoke from electrical panels
Power lines are down near your area
Outage exceeds 24 hours with medical equipment dependency

water.title: Water Scarcity / Purification
water.summary: You die in 3 days without water. Finding and purifying water is your highest survival priority after immediate safety.
steps: Locate any water source: rain, streams, rivers, pipes, morning dew, vegetation
Boil water vigorously for 1 full minute — kills all biological threats (3 minutes at high altitude)
If no fire available: use water purification tablets — iodine or chlorine
SODIS method (no tablets or fire): fill clear plastic bottles, leave in direct sunlight for 6 hours minimum (12 hours if cloudy)
Pre-filter cloudy water through cloth, sand, or charcoal before purifying
Build a simple filter: layer of grass → charcoal (from fire) → sand → gravel in a bottle with holes at bottom
Collect rainwater in any clean container, tarp, or plastic sheet
Morning dew: wipe metal surfaces, leaves, and grass with cloth, wring into container
Minimum survival: 2 liters per person per day — 3–4 liters in heat or with physical exertion
Prioritize hydration for children and elderly — they deteriorate faster
avoid: Do NOT drink seawater — accelerates fatal dehydration
Do NOT drink urine — only if you can distill it first
Do NOT drink floodwater — contaminated with sewage, chemicals, corpses
Do NOT drink from sources near dead animals or industrial areas
Do NOT wait until severely thirsty — by then you are already dehydrated
when_to_seek_help: No urination for 8+ hours — severe dehydration emergency
Sunken eyes, dry mouth, extreme confusion
Water source smells chemical or appears oily/rainbow-colored
Children not urinating for 6+ hours

food.title: Food Scarcity / Foraging / Food Safety
food.summary: You can survive 3 weeks without food. Water and shelter come first. When food is needed, safety over quantity.
steps: Prioritize water over food — always
Ration existing supplies: adults need minimum 1200 calories/day to remain functional
High-calorie priorities: nuts, oils, grains, dried legumes, peanut butter
Universal edibility test for unknown plants: touch to skin, wait 15min; touch to lip, wait 3min; small taste, wait 8 hours
Safe wild foods in most regions: grass seeds (cooked), inner tree bark (pine), cattail roots, dandelion greens
Insects are highly nutritious: grasshoppers, crickets, grubs, ants — remove wings/legs, cook thoroughly
Cook ALL wild foods thoroughly — parasites and bacteria are killed by heat
For food poisoning: rest, hydrate aggressively — do NOT stop vomiting or diarrhea with medication initially (body is purging)
FIFO method: consume oldest food first to minimize waste
Small frequent meals conserve energy better than large infrequent ones
avoid: Do NOT eat foods that smell sour, look slimy, or have visible mold
Do NOT eat unknown mushrooms — too many deadly lookalikes
Do NOT eat brightly colored berries, milky sap, or bitter-tasting plants
Do NOT take anti-diarrheal medication early in food poisoning
Do NOT eat raw meat or fish unless absolutely no other option
when_to_seek_help: Food poisoning with high fever, blood in stool, or neurological symptoms
Children not eating for 24+ hours
Severe weakness or inability to stand from hunger
Allergic reaction: swelling, hives, difficulty breathing

mental.title: Panic / Psychological Crisis / PTSD
mental.summary: Your mind is your most critical survival tool. Managing fear is not weakness — it is the most tactical thing you can do.
steps: STOP moving. Sit or crouch in the safest available position.
Box breathing: Inhale 4 counts → Hold 4 → Exhale 4 → Hold 4 — repeat 6 times
Grounding (5-4-3-2-1): name 5 things you see, 4 you can touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste
Remind yourself: panic attacks physically cannot last more than 20–30 minutes — you will survive this
Focus on ONE single next action only — not the whole situation
Speak out loud — explain your situation step by step, even if alone — externalizes and organizes thought
Physical anchor: press your feet hard into the ground, feel the texture — this activates parasympathetic nervous system
Drink water slowly if available
If with others: physical touch (hand on shoulder) reduces cortisol rapidly
Write down resources, threats, and next steps if paper available — reduces cognitive load
avoid: Do NOT make major decisions during peak panic — wait for baseline
Do NOT isolate yourself from others if avoidable
Do NOT focus on worst-case scenarios
Do NOT consume alcohol to calm down — worsens anxiety, causes dehydration
Do NOT hyperventilate — breathe slowly
when_to_seek_help: Panic lasting more than 30 minutes with chest pain — could be cardiac
Suicidal thoughts or complete dissociation from reality
Person unresponsive to grounding techniques for over an hour
Children in prolonged distress or showing regression in behavior
Flashbacks, inability to function, or violent behavior after trauma

animal_bite.title: Animal Bite Treatment
animal_bite.summary: Animal bites can cause infection and may require urgent medical attention depending on severity and rabies risk.
steps: Wash the wound immediately with soap and running water for at least 10 minutes.
Apply gentle pressure if bleeding.
Use antiseptic if available.
Cover with a clean bandage.
Monitor for redness, swelling, fever, or pus.
avoid: Do not ignore deep puncture wounds.
Do not close infected bites tightly.
Do not delay medical attention for unknown animals.
when_to_seek_help: If the animal was stray or behaving strangely.
If bleeding will not stop.
If signs of infection appear.
If bite is near face, hands, or joints.

solar_power.title: Setting Up Solar Power Off-Grid
solar_power.summary: A basic solar setup can power lights, phone charging, and radio communication indefinitely. This is achievable with minimal technical knowledge.
steps: Identify your power needs first: phone charging = ~10Wh/day, LED light = ~5Wh/night, radio = ~3Wh/day
Basic system components: solar panel → charge controller → battery → load (your devices)
Panel placement: face panels TRUE south (northern hemisphere) or TRUE north (southern hemisphere), tilt angle = your latitude in degrees
Avoid shading — even partial shade on one cell can reduce entire panel output by 50–80%
Connect panel to charge controller first (NEVER connect panel directly to battery — will damage it)
Connect battery to charge controller (observe polarity: red = positive +, black = negative −)
Connect your load/devices to charge controller output terminals
A 20W panel in 4 peak sun hours = 80Wh/day — enough to charge 3–4 phones daily
Improvised connection: car battery clips (jumper cables) can connect panels to car batteries
Monitor battery voltage: 12.7V = full, 12.4V = half, 12.0V = nearly empty — stop drawing at 12.0V or you damage it
Size your battery: if you need 100Wh/day, use minimum 200Ah battery (never discharge below 50%)
avoid: Do NOT connect solar panel directly to battery without a charge controller — overcharging destroys the battery
Do NOT reverse polarity — always double-check + and − before connecting
Do NOT let lead-acid batteries discharge below 50% regularly — shortens life dramatically
Do NOT place panels flat — they need tilt for maximum output and rain self-cleaning
Do NOT leave connections loose — arcing causes fire
when_to_seek_help: Battery gets very hot during charging — possible short circuit
Charge controller shows error codes
Panel voltage reads zero even in direct sunlight — damaged panel or wiring

battery_diy.title: Emergency Battery Pack / Power Storage
battery_diy.summary: While making a true battery from scratch is impractical, you can build functional power systems from salvaged components.
steps: Best source of emergency batteries: car batteries (12V, 40–100Ah), motorcycle batteries (12V, 7–20Ah), or UPS backup batteries
Car battery salvage: can be removed with a 10mm wrench — disconnect negative (black) terminal FIRST, then positive (red)
Series connection (increases voltage): connect + of battery 1 to − of battery 2 → total voltage doubles, capacity stays same
Parallel connection (increases capacity): connect + to + and − to − → total voltage stays same, capacity doubles
Improvised battery from household items (low power, demonstration only): zinc-copper cell using pennies (copper), zinc washers, and saltwater-soaked paper — produces ~0.76V per cell
Lemon/potato battery: insert copper wire and zinc nail into fruit/vegetable — produces ~0.9V per cell (enough to power LED, not useful devices)
For real emergency power: 6 AA batteries in series = 9V, 8 × AA = 12V — can trickle-charge phones via USB step-down converter
18650 lithium cells from old laptop batteries: each provides 3.7V, 2–3Ah — 3 in series = ~11V usable power bank
To extract 18650 cells: open laptop battery pack carefully with flathead screwdriver, cells are cylindrical inside
Build a battery holder: cardboard tube, aluminum foil contacts, tape — hold 18650 or AA cells in series
Charge 18650 cells safely: use dedicated charger or TP4056 module — NEVER charge damaged or swollen cells
avoid: Do NOT short-circuit any battery — lithium batteries can catch fire; lead-acid can explode
Do NOT charge lithium batteries with a car battery charger — wrong voltage, will cause fire
Do NOT use damaged, swollen, or leaking batteries — toxic and fire risk
Do NOT connect batteries of different capacities or chemistries in parallel
Do NOT heat any battery to force charge — highly dangerous
when_to_seek_help: Battery becomes hot to touch during use or charging
Battery swells, leaks, or emits chemical smell
Spark or smoke from connections

penicillin_antibiotics.title: Wound Infection / Improvised Antibiotics
penicillin_antibiotics.summary: True home penicillin production is not reliable. But there are proven methods to fight infection with accessible materials.
steps: CLEAN THE WOUND FIRST — this prevents infection better than any antibiotic: irrigate with clean water under pressure (use syringe, bottle with pinhole cap, or squeeze bottle) for at least 5 full minutes
Use clean tweezers sterilized in flame to remove visible debris or foreign material
Hydrogen peroxide (3%): use ONCE on initial cleaning only — kills bacteria but also damages healing tissue if used repeatedly
Honey (raw, unprocessed): apply directly to wound — has genuine proven antibacterial properties (hydrogen peroxide-producing enzymes), cover with clean cloth
Sugar packing: pack wound with dry granulated sugar — creates osmotic environment that kills bacteria, change daily
Garlic: crush 2–3 raw garlic cloves, apply paste to wound edges (not inside deep wounds) — allicin has documented antibacterial effect
Turmeric paste: mix turmeric powder with clean water, apply to wound surface — curcumin has anti-inflammatory and mild antibacterial properties
Signs of spreading infection requiring systemic treatment: red streaks spreading from wound, fever over 38.5°C, severe swelling, pus with foul odor
If tetracycline/doxycycline or amoxicillin is available (fish/veterinary antibiotics in emergency): standard dose doxycycline is 100mg twice daily for 7–10 days
Prevent infection by keeping wound covered, dry between cleanings, and elevated when possible
True penicillin from mold (Penicillium chrysogenum — blue-green mold on citrus): requires controlled fermentation, extraction, and dosing — NOT reliably possible in field conditions
avoid: Do NOT close a dirty wound — infected wounds must drain, closure traps bacteria
Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide repeatedly on a wound — damages healing tissue
Do NOT use motor oil, mud, ash, or animal dung on wounds — causes severe infection
Do NOT ignore red streaks spreading from a wound — this is sepsis beginning, immediately life-threatening
Do NOT stop antibiotic treatment early even if feeling better — resistance develops
when_to_seek_help: Red streaks extending from wound — early sepsis, needs IV antibiotics urgently
Fever above 38.5°C associated with the wound
Wound not improving after 48 hours of treatment
Green or black tissue visible — tissue death (necrosis)
Person becomes confused or lethargic alongside wound infection — septic shock

shelter.title: Emergency Shelter Construction
shelter.summary: You die in 3 hours in cold wet wind without shelter. Building one is your top priority in any outdoor survival situation.
steps: Location selection: avoid flood plains, hilltops (lightning), dead trees, animal trails, and areas directly below cliffs
Ideal site: natural windbreak (large rocks, dense trees), near but not in water, sloped slightly for drainage
Fastest shelter (15 minutes): lean two large branches against a tree trunk or wall at 45°, cover with smaller branches, pile leaves and debris over it at least 60cm thick for insulation
Debris hut (best solo warmth): make a pile of leaves and debris 1 meter deep, crawl inside — body heat trapped inside warm air is remarkable
Tarp/plastic sheet shelter: tie one edge high between two trees, stake other edge to ground at 45° angle — effective rain and wind protection
Snow shelter (quinzhee): pile snow into 2m mound, wait 2 hours for sintering, hollow out leaving 20cm walls — interior stays near 0°C even at −40°C outside
Insulate the floor — ground pulls heat from your body faster than cold air — use leaves, grass, clothing, anything to create a barrier
Insulation rule: the bigger your shelter, the harder to heat — build it just large enough for your body and essential gear
Fire placement: if using fire for warmth, position reflective surface (rocks, metal) behind fire to direct heat toward shelter entrance
avoid: Do NOT build shelter in dry riverbeds — flash floods give no warning
Do NOT use green (living) wood for tinder — it does not burn
Do NOT let shelter interior get wet — wet insulation loses 90% of its effectiveness
Do NOT rely on shelter without checking structural stability — test before sleeping inside
when_to_seek_help: Someone is showing signs of hypothermia: uncontrolled shivering, confusion, slurred speech — immediate warmth emergency
Structural danger: mudslide risk, flood rising, or structural instability

fire_making.title: Starting Fire Without Modern Tools
fire_making.summary: Fire provides warmth, water purification, signaling, and psychological comfort. Master at least two methods.
steps: Always collect 3 types of material before attempting ignition: tinder (cotton, dry grass, paper, bark dust), kindling (pencil-width sticks), fuel (thumb-to-wrist diameter logs)
Friction method — bow drill: carve a straight dry spindle (~45cm), flat fireboard with a notch, and a simple bow with paracord or shoelace
Bow drill technique: place spindle in fireboard notch, bow horizontal, press down with hand socket, push bow back and forth rapidly — generates ember in notch
Transfer ember to tinder bundle: gently tip ember into loose ball of dry grass or bark fiber, fold bundle around it, blow gently until it ignites
Flint and steel: strike sharp flint edge against high-carbon steel at steep angle (45°) — sparks ignite dry char cloth or tinder fungus
Battery and steel wool: touch fine steel wool to both terminals of any battery (9V works best, but even phone battery works) — steel wool ignites instantly
Magnifying glass/phone camera lens/eyeglasses lens: focus sunlight to a pinpoint on dark tinder — works in 30–60 seconds on a sunny day
Chocolate and can method: polish bottom of aluminum can with chocolate (abrasive) until mirror-bright, use as parabolic reflector to focus sunlight
Fire structure: build a small pyramid of kindling over your tinder, add fuel logs in a star or teepee pattern — do not smother your initial flame with large wood too early
Keep your fire going: maintain a base of coals — these are more valuable than flame and can re-ignite new wood
avoid: Do NOT use wet or green wood — you will fail and waste energy
Do NOT use all your dry tinder on your first attempt — prepare multiple bundles
Do NOT build fire in enclosed spaces without ventilation — carbon monoxide risk
Do NOT light fire in windy conditions without a windbreak
Do NOT pour flammable liquid on existing fire — it flares unpredictably
when_to_seek_help: Hypothermia is occurring and fire cannot be started — move to shelter and use body heat between people immediately

navigation.title: Navigation Without GPS or Compass
navigation.summary: You can determine direction with only the sun, stars, a watch, or natural signs. Staying oriented can save your life.
steps: Sun method (Northern Hemisphere): sun rises in the EAST, sets in the WEST — at solar noon (highest point) it is DUE SOUTH
Shadow stick method: place a 1m stick vertically, mark the tip of its shadow, wait 15 minutes, mark again — the line from first to second mark points EAST
Watch method: point the hour hand at the sun — the midpoint between hour hand and 12 o'clock is south (Northern Hemisphere); use the 12 and midpoint to find south
Night navigation by stars: find Polaris (North Star) — locate the Big Dipper (Ursa Major), extend the line from the two outer stars of the cup 5 times — that is Polaris (true north)
Southern Hemisphere: find Southern Cross constellation, extend the long axis 4.5 times from the bottom star — that is south
Vegetation clues: moss grows predominantly on north-facing sides (less sunlight) in Northern Hemisphere; trees tend to have more branches on their south side
River navigation: rivers flow downhill toward lower terrain and eventually to larger water bodies or inhabited areas
Create a mental map: always note landmarks (hills, rivers, distinctive trees) relative to your direction of travel
Signal your position: if rescue is possible, stay where you are and signal rather than walk randomly — three fires in a triangle is universal distress signal
Leave trail markers: stack rocks, break branches, or mark trees if moving — so rescuers can follow, and you can backtrack
avoid: Do NOT travel in complete darkness in unknown terrain — injuries from falls are high
Do NOT navigate by a single sign — confirm direction with multiple methods
Do NOT assume a phone compass is accurate near metal structures or electrical equipment
when_to_seek_help: Completely disoriented with no landmarks — stay put and signal
Nightfall approaching in unknown wilderness without shelter

chemical_attack.title: Chemical Attack / Toxic Gas Exposure
chemical_attack.summary: Chemical weapons are immediately life-threatening. Getting upwind and decontaminating within minutes is critical.
steps: MOVE IMMEDIATELY: get upwind of the source — chemicals drift downwind, move 90° to wind direction then upwind
Get to high ground if possible — most chemical agents are heavier than air and settle in low areas
Improvised respiratory protection: wet cloth (multiple layers) over mouth and nose filters some chemical agents — not perfect but provides some protection
Cover any skin exposure: cover exposed arms, hands, neck — chemical agents absorb through skin
Decontamination (within 1–2 minutes is critical): remove ALL clothing by cutting off (pulling over head spreads contamination to face)
Flush skin and eyes with large amounts of clean water for 15 minutes minimum — do not use hot water (increases absorption)
Chlorine/industrial gas exposure: move to fresh air, breathe slowly and shallowly, do not exert yourself
Nerve agent signs: pinpoint pupils, drooling, muscle twitching, seizures, loss of bladder control — requires atropine (military autoinjector if available)
Blister agent signs: burning skin/eyes appearing 2–24 hours after exposure — do not burst blisters, cover with clean dressing
Help others decontaminate before they enter clean areas — do not let contaminated individuals spread agent to others
avoid: Do NOT go to low-lying areas — heavier-than-air gases concentrate there
Do NOT stay in a downwind position from the source
Do NOT remove a gas mask before confirming you are in clean air
Do NOT touch victims of chemical attack without protection — you can become a secondary casualty
Do NOT induce vomiting if a chemical agent was swallowed
when_to_seek_help: Any suspected nerve agent exposure — requires antidote within minutes
Respiratory distress after chemical exposure
Seizures, loss of consciousness, or severe burning of eyes and skin
Multiple casualties showing same symptoms simultaneously

explosive_landmine.title: Landmine / IED / Explosive Hazard Awareness
explosive_landmine.summary: The only safe action near suspected explosives is distance. Your instincts will be wrong — do not approach, touch, or run.
steps: FREEZE immediately if you suspect you have entered a mined area — look at the ground around your feet before moving
Do NOT run — running increases footfall pressure and reduces your ability to spot hazards
If you suspect a landmine field: look for disturbed earth, unusual mounds, trip wires, unusual objects, or sudden gaps in vegetation
Move back the way you came using your EXACT footsteps — step only where you have already walked safely
If you cannot retrace steps: shuffle forward very slowly, probing the ground ahead with a stick at 45° angle before each step
Mark the area visibly: tie cloth to trees, stack rocks, mark with anything visible — to warn others
If someone triggers a mine: do NOT rush toward them — you may trigger additional mines placed around the first
Apply tourniquets and pressure to blast wounds from a safe distance using thrown materials if possible, or approach only after confirming the immediate area is clear
Treat blast injury: control hemorrhage immediately, shock position, check for secondary blast injuries (internal bleeding, hearing damage, eye damage)
Unexploded ordnance (UXO): mark and report — NEVER touch, move, or attempt to defuse
Suspicious object protocol: recognize, avoid, report — these are the only three actions
avoid: Do NOT touch or approach any unexploded device
Do NOT assume an area is safe because others have walked through it — mines can have delayed triggers
Do NOT use metal tools to probe for mines — stick only
Do NOT use radio or mobile phone near suspected IEDs — radio signals can trigger some devices
Do NOT attempt to defuse any explosive device under any circumstances
when_to_seek_help: Immediately after any blast injury — internal injuries are common and not always visible
Any time you are in a suspected mined area — mark it and evacuate
If someone is trapped and cannot be safely reached

gunshot_wound.title: Gunshot Wound — Tactical First Aid
gunshot_wound.summary: Gunshot wounds kill through bleeding and collapsed lungs. Control bleeding in the first 3 minutes or the chance of survival drops dramatically.
steps: First: ensure the threat is over — you cannot help anyone if you are also shot
Get the casualty behind cover before treating
Assess: check for multiple wounds — entry and exit wounds, hidden wounds under clothing
Limb wounds: apply tourniquet immediately, high and tight (as high on the limb as possible), tighten until bleeding stops, mark time
Trunk/chest wound: listen for sucking sound when breathing — this indicates open chest wound (pneumothorax)
Seal open chest wounds with chest seal (or improvised: plastic wrapper, credit card, tape on 3 sides to allow air out but not in)
Abdominal wound: do NOT pack with pressure — keep intestines moist with clean cloth if exposed, cover loosely
Pack high/deep wounds (armpit, groin, neck) with gauze or cloth — push DEEP into wound cavity with your fingers and maintain firm pressure for at least 3 minutes without releasing
Watch for tension pneumothorax: breathing becomes increasingly labored, trachea deviates — requires needle decompression if trained
Shock treatment: lay flat, elevate legs if no spinal/chest injury suspected, keep warm
Do NOT remove bullet — hospital procedure only; focus on bleeding control
avoid: Do NOT remove your hands from a packed wound to check if bleeding stopped — minimum 3 minutes uninterrupted pressure
Do NOT give food or water to someone with abdominal wound
Do NOT ignore small entry wounds — bullets can travel internally and cause massive internal damage
Do NOT place airtight seal on both sides of chest wound — must allow air to escape on one side
when_to_seek_help: Every gunshot wound requires surgical evaluation — even wounds that appear minor
Increasing breathing difficulty after chest wound
Uncontrolled bleeding after tourniquet application
Decreased consciousness, rapid weak pulse — hemorrhagic shock

water_purification_advanced.title: Advanced Water Purification Methods
water_purification_advanced.summary: Multiple purification methods combined are always safer than one alone. Master at least three of these.
steps: Method 1 — Boiling: the gold standard — 1 minute rolling boil kills everything biological (not chemical)
Method 2 — Chlorine bleach: add 8 drops of unscented household bleach (5–6% sodium hypochlorite) per 4 liters, wait 30 minutes before drinking
Method 3 — Iodine tablets: 1 tablet per liter, wait 30 minutes (60 minutes if cold or cloudy water)
Method 4 — SODIS: fill clear PET plastic bottles, lay on reflective surface in full sun for 6 hours minimum
Method 5 — Ceramic pot filter: red clay + combustible material (sawdust, straw) fired in kiln removes 99.9% of bacteria
Method 6 — Solar still: dig a pit 1m wide, 60cm deep in moist ground; place container in center; cover with plastic sheet; weight center down with a stone — condensation drips into container
Method 7 — Distillation: boil water, collect steam in a cloth over the pot, wring cloth into container — removes chemicals AND biological threats
Pre-filtration (always do first): let water settle for 1 hour; then pour through cloth/sand/charcoal before chemical or UV treatment
Biochar filter build: crush charred wood into fine powder, pack tightly in a container with holes — effective pre-filter for removing larger contaminants and improving taste
UV pen sterilizers: if available, run for 60 seconds per liter, stir while treating — kills all biological threats without chemicals
avoid: Do NOT use scented bleach — fragrance chemicals are toxic
Do NOT use iodine purification for more than 3 weeks continuously — thyroid effects
Do NOT use SODIS in colored plastic bottles — UV does not penetrate
Do NOT assume any single method covers everything — boiling kills biology, not chemicals
when_to_seek_help: Water source appears chemically contaminated — oil sheen, chemical smell, unusual color — no purification method makes this safe
Person showing signs of waterborne illness despite treatment

radio_communication.title: Emergency Radio Communication
radio_communication.summary: Radio works when phones fail. Even basic radios can reach rescue services across hundreds of kilometers.
steps: Emergency frequencies to know: AM 1000kHz (news/info), FM 88–108MHz (local), VHF 156.8MHz (maritime distress), UHF 243.0MHz (military/aviation emergency)
International distress frequencies: 121.5 MHz (aviation emergency), 156.800 MHz (marine channel 16), 27.065 MHz (CB radio channel 9 — designated emergency)
If you have any AM/FM radio: keep it tuned to local news stations — emergency broadcasts continue even when internet fails
Handheld walkie-talkie/FRS radio: channel 1 at power outage or disaster — most common emergency channel
Extend antenna: more antenna length = more range — connect a length of wire to the antenna terminal of any radio
Improvised antenna: run a long wire (minimum 15m) as high as possible in a straight line for AM reception
Morse code for emergency signaling: SOS = three short, three long, three short (· · · − − − · · ·) — transmit on any frequency
If no radio: visual signals — mirror flashing is visible 15+ km; fire + green vegetation = white smoke (day); fire alone = visible at night for 30+ km
Mobile phone: even without a carrier signal, all phones can call emergency services if any network is detectable
Satellite communicators (Garmin inReach, SPOT): if available, these work anywhere on Earth
avoid: Do NOT transmit on military or aviation frequencies unless genuine emergency — serious legal consequences and you may interfere with rescue operations
Do NOT keep transmitting without listening — listen first to find active channels
Do NOT deplete radio batteries transmitting when no one is nearby — transmit periodically and monitor
when_to_seek_help: When contact with emergency services has been established — follow their instructions precisely
When communication is established, provide: location, number of people, injuries, resources needed

hypothermia.title: Hypothermia / Cold Exposure
hypothermia.summary: Hypothermia begins when core temperature drops below 35°C. It impairs judgment before it causes pain — the victim often doesn't know how serious it is.
steps: Remove the person from cold environment — get them to shelter immediately
Remove ALL wet clothing — wet fabric accelerates heat loss 25x faster than dry
Insulate from ground first — ground pulls heat faster than cold air
Cover with as many dry insulating layers as available — sleeping bag, blankets, clothing, leaves
Apply heat to the core: warm (not hot) water bottles or warm packs to armpits, groin, and neck — these are major blood vessel areas
Share body heat: if multiple people, have a warm person lie close and share body heat
Give warm sweet drinks if the person is conscious and can swallow safely — hot chocolate, tea, warm water with sugar
Handle gently — rapid movement can trigger fatal heart arrhythmia in severe hypothermia
Frostbite treatment: rewarm ONLY if no risk of re-freezing (re-freezing causes more damage than leaving it frozen)
Frostbite rewarming: immerse in warm water 37–40°C for 20–30 minutes — do not rub, do not use direct heat
Signs of mild hypothermia: shivering, clumsiness, slurred speech — manageable
Signs of severe hypothermia: shivering STOPS, confusion, muscle rigidity — life-threatening, handle with extreme care
avoid: Do NOT rub frostbitten tissue — causes additional cellular damage
Do NOT give alcohol — it dilates blood vessels and accelerates heat loss
Do NOT use direct heat (fire, heating pad directly on skin) — skin is numb and will burn
Do NOT rewarm limbs if the person still has to walk to safety — refreezing causes worse damage
Do NOT assume death in a cold victim — people have survived cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia
when_to_seek_help: Person stops shivering but is still cold — paradoxical undressing and confusion follow
Cardiac arrhythmia — irregular or absent pulse
Severe frostbite: hard, white, waxy skin with blisters

bone_fracture.title: Fracture / Bone Break — Field Treatment
bone_fracture.summary: Immobilization is the goal. A well-splinted fracture reduces pain, prevents further injury, and allows safe movement.
steps: Do NOT attempt to straighten a broken bone unless circulation is cut off (no pulse below fracture, blue/pale skin) — if so, gentle traction may be required
Splint the limb in the position you found it — do not force movement
Improvised splint materials: straight sticks, boards, cardboard, rolled magazines/newspapers, trekking poles, tent poles
Splint must extend past the joints ABOVE and BELOW the fracture
Pad the splint generously with cloth, leaves, or foam before securing
Secure with bandages, strips of clothing, paracord, or belts — snug but NOT tight enough to restrict circulation
Check 5 Ps every 15 minutes: Pain, Pallor (skin color), Pulselessness, Paresthesia (numbness/tingling), Paralysis — any of these = splint too tight
Elevate the injured limb above heart level to reduce swelling
Open fracture (bone visible through skin): cover with cleanest available cloth, do NOT try to push bone back — treat as open wound priority
Suspected spinal injury: do NOT move unless absolutely necessary — immobilize head and neck in found position
Pelvis fracture: bind the pelvis with a sheet or belt, keep person flat — can bleed internally 3–4 liters
avoid: Do NOT try to 'pop' or reduce dislocations without proper training — can cause nerve and vascular damage
Do NOT splint so tightly it cuts off circulation
Do NOT ignore pelvis/spinal fractures — often fatal if mishandled
when_to_seek_help: Open fracture — infection risk and major bleeding
Suspected spinal fracture — do not move without proper cervical collar and spinal board
Signs of major internal bleeding: pelvis or femur fracture, increasing shock symptoms

burns.title: Burns — Assessment and Treatment
burns.summary: Cool the burn, protect from infection, manage shock. Never underestimate burn size — large burns are immediately life-threatening.
steps: Remove person from burn source — if clothing is burning, use stop-drop-roll
Remove burning or contaminated clothing and jewelry — but do NOT remove if stuck to skin
Cool the burn: run cool (not cold) clean water over the burn for 20 minutes minimum — start this within 3 hours of injury
Do NOT use ice — causes tissue damage and hypothermia in large burns
Cover with clean non-fluffy material (cling wrap if available, otherwise clean cloth) — loosely, do not wrap tightly
Assess burn size using Rule of Nines: head = 9%, each arm = 9%, chest = 18%, back = 18%, each leg = 18%, genitals = 1%
Burns over 20% of body = major burn emergency — aggressive fluid replacement required
Oral fluids for conscious burn victim: 1 liter per hour in the first few hours (burns cause massive fluid loss)
Chemical burn: brush off dry chemical first, then flush with large amounts of water for 30 minutes
Electrical burn: disconnect source first — the entry wound is small but there may be massive internal tissue destruction along the current path
Do NOT burst blisters intentionally — they protect against infection
avoid: Do NOT apply butter, oil, toothpaste, or any cream to burns — traps heat and causes infection
Do NOT use ice or ice water
Do NOT remove material stuck to burned skin
Do NOT burst blisters
when_to_seek_help: Burns to face, hands, feet, genitals, or over major joints
Burns covering more than 10% body surface
Electrical burns — always require hospital evaluation
Burns in children under 5 or elderly over 60 — thresholds are lower
Smoke inhalation — singed nasal hairs, hoarse voice, carbonaceous sputum

sanitation.title: Emergency Sanitation and Disease Prevention
sanitation.summary: More people die from disease caused by poor sanitation than from the disaster itself. Manage waste properly from day one.
steps: Establish a latrine at least 60 meters from any water source, shelter, or food preparation area — downhill from your water source
Dig cat holes at minimum 15cm deep for individual use — cover with soil after each use to prevent fly access
Group latrine: dig trench 30cm wide, 60cm deep, 1–3m long — cover with soil after each use
Hand hygiene without water: ash from fire rubbed on hands then rinsed with minimal water removes more bacteria than you'd expect
Improvised hand sanitizer: 2 parts isopropyl alcohol (70%+) to 1 part aloe gel or glycerin
Protect food from flies: cover all food immediately after preparation, keep food storage elevated and enclosed
Dispose of dead animals downhill and downwind from camp, buried at least 60cm deep — disease risk from decomposition
Control insects: long sleeves/pants, mosquito netting, apply mud or plant oils (eucalyptus, clove) to exposed skin if chemical repellent unavailable
Oral rehydration solution (ORS): 1 liter water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt — for diarrheal disease, give continuously
Prevent cholera and typhoid: purify ALL water, wash hands before eating, cook food thoroughly, especially meat and eggs
avoid: Do NOT defecate near water sources under any circumstances
Do NOT let flies land on food and then eat it
Do NOT bury waste too shallow — animals will dig it up
when_to_seek_help: Rice-water diarrhea (cholera): watery white stool — can kill by dehydration in hours
Bloody diarrhea with fever (dysentery) — requires antibiotics
Multiple people showing same gastrointestinal symptoms — indicates contaminated water or food source

childbirth_emergency.title: Emergency Childbirth
childbirth_emergency.summary: Most births, even without medical assistance, are natural and successful. Stay calm, keep clean, and prepare to receive the baby.
steps: Time contractions: when contractions are 5 minutes apart and lasting 1 minute, birth is likely within hours
Prepare clean surface: lay down the cleanest available sheets, towels, or clothing
Wash hands thoroughly or use gloves if available
Position: semi-reclined, on hands and knees, or squatting — whatever is most comfortable for the mother
Encourage pushing only during contractions — rest between contractions conserves energy
As baby crowns (head visible): support the head gently with your hand — do NOT pull
If amniotic sac is still intact over baby's face: gently rupture with a finger
After head delivers: check for umbilical cord around neck — if present, slip it over the head gently
Baby follows naturally — slight downward guidance of the head helps the shoulders deliver
Baby should cry and breathe immediately — if not: rub back firmly, flick the soles of the feet
Keep baby warm immediately — skin-to-skin on mother's chest covered with clothing/blanket
Placenta delivers 5–30 minutes after baby — do NOT pull the cord — allow natural delivery
Umbilical cord: tie tightly with clean string/thread at 3cm and 5cm from baby, cut between ties with cleanest available tool
avoid: Do NOT pull on the baby's head — guide only, allow natural movement
Do NOT pull the umbilical cord to deliver placenta
Do NOT cut the cord until it stops pulsating if possible
Do NOT give mother anything to eat until placenta has delivered
when_to_seek_help: Heavy bleeding (soaking more than one pad per 5 minutes) after delivery
Baby does not breathe after 1 minute of stimulation — begin infant CPR
Placenta does not deliver within 60 minutes
Umbilical cord delivers before baby (cord prolapse) — emergency, head to knees position immediately

signal_rescue.title: Signaling for Rescue
signal_rescue.summary: Being found is a skill. Rescuers cannot help you if they cannot find you — visibility and pattern are everything.
steps: Stay near your last known position — rescuers begin search from your last known location
Three is the universal distress signal: 3 fires, 3 whistle blasts, 3 gunshots, 3 flashes of light — any three repeating signals
Ground-to-air signals: lay out large letters in open ground using rocks, logs, or trampled grass: 'X' = need medical assistance, 'V' = need help, '→' = going this direction
Make signals large: minimum 10m long to be visible from aircraft — bigger is always better
Smoke signals (day): add green leaves, rubber, or oil to fire for black or white smoke — smoke visible 30+ km in clear conditions
Fire signals (night): open flame on a hilltop is visible 30+ km — build 3 fires in triangle pattern
Mirror/reflective surface: a signal mirror can be seen 160km away by aircraft — aim the reflection at aircraft by creating a V with your fingers and flashing light through it
Orange tarp or bright clothing: lay flat on open ground in a clearing — color contrast against natural terrain is visible from aircraft
Whistle: 3 blasts = distress, pause, repeat — carry on your person at all times in field conditions
If you must move: leave clear direction markers and arrows showing your direction of travel, with dates
avoid: Do NOT signal from inside forest or under tree canopy — not visible from air
Do NOT light signal fire in dry conditions without firebreak — creates wildfire risk
Do NOT use all fuel for signal fire before nightfall
when_to_seek_help: When you are in a safe location and rescuers are potentially active — signal continuously

electricity_generation.title: Alternative Electricity Generation
electricity_generation.summary: Multiple low-tech methods can generate small but critical amounts of electricity for communication and lighting.
steps: Hand-crank generator: many emergency radios and flashlights have built-in hand cranks — 1 minute of cranking ≈ 5–10 minutes of use
Bicycle generator: attach a small DC motor (from printer, blender, or toy) to bicycle wheel — spinning at 300 RPM generates 5–12V
Bike setup: mount motor so its shaft presses against the tire, connect motor leads to a battery via a diode (prevents backflow) — commercial versions called dynamos
Micro-hydro: if you have flowing water, a turbine (improvised from a bucket with holes, windmill blades, or barrel) can generate continuous DC power
Wind turbine basics: a motor run in reverse generates electricity — mount on a high pole, connect blades (cut from PVC pipe or sheet metal), connect to battery through a diode

Thermoelectric generator (TEG): placing a Peltier module between a hot and cold surface generates small amounts of electricity — fire on one side, cold metal on other — enough to charge a phone over several hours
Steam power: a simple steam-driven turbine can power a generator — requires sealed boiler, turbine blades, and generator — complex but viable for extended survival
Car alternator: removed from a vehicle, driven by any rotating power source (bicycle, water wheel, wind) — produces 12–14V at sufficient RPM (typically 1500–2000 RPM)
avoid: Do NOT connect a generator directly to devices without voltage regulation — voltage spikes destroy electronics
Do NOT build steam systems without pressure relief — explosions risk
Do NOT run a gasoline generator in any enclosed space
when_to_seek_help: Electrical burns or shock from improvised systems — always treat as serious

food_preservation.title: Food Preservation Without Refrigeration
food_preservation.summary: Properly preserved food lasts months or years. These methods have sustained civilizations for millennia.
steps: Drying/dehydration: slice food thin (5mm), expose to sun and hot air with good airflow — fruit dries in 2–4 days, meat in 1–3 days; food is ready when brittle with no moisture when squeezed
Salt curing (meat and fish): pack in coarse salt (at least 30g salt per 100g meat), leave for 24–48 hours, then rinse and dry — preserved for weeks
Smoking: cold smoke (below 30°C) at 8–12 hours preserves for weeks; hot smoke (60–80°C) at 2–4 hours preserves for days; combine with salt curing for maximum shelf life
Smoking setup: enclosed space, small fire producing lots of smoke (green wood, sawdust), food hanging above not in direct heat
Fermentation: vegetables submerged in 2% salt brine (20g salt per liter water) ferment safely and are preserved for months — sauerkraut, kimchi principle
Pickling: submerge in vinegar (5% acidity) — preserves most foods for 1–12 months
Fat preservation (confit): submerge cooked meat completely in rendered fat (lard, oil) — traditional method preserves for months without refrigeration
Root cellar principle: bury food in earth at least 60cm deep — ground temperature stays 10–15°C, dramatically slowing spoilage
Clay pot cooling: place clay pot inside a larger clay pot, fill gap between with wet sand, cover with wet cloth — evaporative cooling keeps interior 15–20°C below ambient
Honey preservation: raw honey never spoils — food submerged in honey is preserved indefinitely
avoid: Do NOT partially dry food and stop — moisture remaining allows mold and botulism growth
Do NOT smoke without salt curing first for meats — not always sufficient alone
Do NOT seal fermented foods airtight early — CO2 must escape or jars will explode
when_to_seek_help: Botulism symptoms after eating preserved food: double vision, difficulty swallowing, muscle weakness — extremely serious, often fatal without treatment

medicine_improvised.title: Improvised and Natural Medicine
medicine_improvised.summary: Natural plant-based medicines have real pharmacological effects. These are not myths — they are the basis of modern pharmaceuticals.
steps: Willow bark (Salix species — any willow tree): contains salicin, the natural form of aspirin — chew small piece of inner bark, or boil bark in water 20 minutes, drink the tea — fever reducer and pain reliever
Garlic: proven antibacterial — eat raw crushed garlic or apply topically to infected wounds
Turmeric: anti-inflammatory — mix with water as paste for external application on inflamed or infected tissue
Ginger: antinausea — chew fresh ginger root or boil in water for nausea, digestive upset, motion sickness
Plantain (Plantago major — common weed, oval ribbed leaves): chew into paste and apply to wounds, burns, insect stings — draws out toxins, reduces inflammation
Yarrow (Achillea millefolium — feathery leaves, white flowers): apply crushed leaves to cuts and wounds — natural hemostatic (stops bleeding) and antibacterial
Activated charcoal (from fire): crush completely burned wood/bone into fine black powder, mix with water, drink — absorbs many poisons and toxins (not effective for all)
Pine needle tea: high in vitamin C — boil green pine needles 10 minutes, strain and drink — prevents scurvy in extended survival
Aloe vera (if available): split leaf and apply gel directly to burns, sunburn, skin irritation — soothing and antibacterial
Honey: apply to wounds and burns — proven antibacterial and promotes wound healing
avoid: Do NOT use any plant medicine you cannot positively identify — misidentification causes poisoning
Do NOT use willow bark if allergic to aspirin
Do NOT give willow bark to children under 16 — Reye's syndrome risk (same as aspirin)
Do NOT rely on natural remedies for life-threatening infections requiring antibiotics
when_to_seek_help: Any poisoning or allergic reaction after consuming any plant
Infection worsening despite treatment
Symptoms of serious disease requiring actual antibiotics or surgery

water_collection.title: Water Collection From Environment
water_collection.summary: Water exists in nearly every environment if you know where to look. These techniques can sustain life when no obvious source is available.
steps: Rain collection: spread any waterproof material (tarp, plastic sheet, large leaves) and channel into container — a 1m² tarp in moderate rain collects 1 liter per hour
Morning dew: before sunrise, wipe metal surfaces, vehicles, leaves, and grass with an absorbent cloth, wring into container — 500ml to 2 liters possible per morning
Transpiration bag: tie a large clear plastic bag over a leafy tree branch in morning sun — water vapor from leaves condenses inside — collect 250–500ml per bag per day
Plant sources: cacti (not all safe), certain vines — cut vine near ground, hold up and let water drain from cut end
Green bamboo: cut segment and shake — young bamboo segments hold water
Digging for water: look for lowest points in landscape, dried riverbeds, base of cliffs — dig until you hit damp soil, allow water to seep into hole and collect
Follow animals at dawn and dusk — they travel to water sources; follow birds flying at low altitude in straight lines
Fog collection: string mesh or cloth between poles in foggy areas — fog droplets condense and drip down into container; commercially used in desert communities
Beach/coastal: dig above high tide line in wet sand — you may find less saline water seeping through sand, purify before drinking
Ice and snow: melt before consuming — eating snow lowers core temperature; 1kg snow = approximately 100ml water
avoid: Do NOT eat ice or snow without melting first — causes hypothermia
Do NOT cut saguaro or other toxic cacti — barrel cactus juice is actually emetic
Do NOT collect water runoff from metal roofs with lead-based paint
when_to_seek_help: When collection is impossible and dehydration is progressing — movement toward known water source may be necessary

wound_closure.title: Emergency Wound Closure
wound_closure.summary: Not all wounds need sutures. Proper closure technique prevents infection and aids healing.
steps: First: clean the wound thoroughly — irrigate under pressure with clean water for at minimum 5 minutes
Assess: wounds older than 6 hours, heavily contaminated, or showing signs of infection should NOT be closed — leave open to drain
Butterfly closure strips (improvised): cut strips of tape with narrow center and wider ends, place across wound edges, pulling them together
Improvised steri-strips: cut medical tape or duct tape into thin strips, apply perpendicular to the wound
Wound glue (super glue/cyanoacrylate): hold wound edges together firmly, apply thin layer of super glue over top (not inside the wound) — do not use inside deep wounds or near eyes

Simple interrupted suture technique (if suture materials available): insert needle 5mm from wound edge, pass through all skin layers, exit 5mm on other side, tie with square knot (left over right, then right over left)
Spacing: place sutures every 5–8mm along the wound length
After closure: apply thin layer of antibiotic ointment if available, cover with clean dressing
Change dressing daily: clean wound, inspect for infection signs (redness spreading, swelling increasing, pus, foul smell)
Suture removal: simple sutures on face: 5–7 days; extremities: 10–14 days; over joints: 14 days
avoid: Do NOT close a contaminated or infected wound — traps bacteria and causes abscess
Do NOT close animal bite wounds — infection risk too high
Do NOT close wounds if there is any foreign material still inside
Do NOT over-tighten sutures — should approximate edges, not strangle tissue
when_to_seek_help: Any wound closure over a joint, face, or genitals should be seen by medical professional if possible
Any sign of infection developing in a closed wound — open it and let it drain
Tendon, nerve, or bone visible in wound — complex repair required

building_defenses.title: Improvised Defensive Positions and Protection
building_defenses.summary: In conflict zones, understanding cover vs concealment and hardening your position can save lives.
steps: Cover vs concealment: Cover stops bullets (concrete, engine blocks, filled sandbags, packed earth); concealment only hides you (bushes, thin walls, wood)
Best cover materials ranked: packed earth/sandbags (best), concrete/masonry, brick, engine block, large trees (hardwood), filled water containers (water stops bullets)
Sandbag wall: fill bags 2/3 full with damp earth (damp sand holds shape better), stack in staggered brick pattern, minimum 60cm thickness for rifle protection
Improvised sandbags: use any fabric bag, backpack, canvas — fill with soil, sand, or gravel
Safe room in house: choose interior room (no exterior walls), reinforce walls with heavy furniture, books, sandbags — concrete stairs are excellent cover
Window protection: stack books/sandbags along lower half of window interior — leave only small gap to see through
Floor vs upper floors: ground floor is safer — upper floors are silhouetted and harder to exit under fire; roof positions are exposed
Observation with minimal exposure: observe around corners (not over them), at ground level, using a mirror if available
Noise and light discipline at night: cover all light sources, move quietly, assume any light or noise reveals your position
Entry point control: add multiple locks, reinforce door frames with additional wood or metal, use door wedges from inside
avoid: Do NOT assume wood walls, standard doors, or thin metal stops bullets
Do NOT position yourself in front of windows — frame yourself against a wall beside the window
Do NOT create positions that have only one exit route
Do NOT expose yourself unnecessarily to assess threats
when_to_seek_help: Under active fire — evacuate to safer position if possible
Position compromised — fall back to secondary position

dehydration_treatment.title: Severe Dehydration / ORS Treatment
dehydration_treatment.summary: Dehydration kills through organ failure. Oral rehydration is as effective as IV drip if the person can drink — start immediately.
steps: ORS formula: 1 liter of purified water + 6 level teaspoons of sugar + 1/2 level teaspoon of salt — stir until dissolved
Give small sips continuously — 200–400ml per hour for mild/moderate dehydration, 1 liter per hour for severe
Do NOT give only plain water — this dilutes electrolytes further and can cause hyponatremia (water intoxication)
Coconut water (fresh, unprocessed) is an excellent natural ORS — contains electrolytes in good balance
Assess severity: mild = dry mouth, slightly dark urine; moderate = very dark urine, dizziness; severe = no urine, confusion, sunken eyes, rapid weak pulse
For vomiting: give 5ml every 2–3 minutes — even if vomiting continues some is absorbed
Position: laying flat with legs elevated helps blood flow to vital organs in severe dehydration
Food can wait — fluids are the only priority until urine output resumes and person is alert
Rehydration salt sachets (WHO/UNICEF standard): 2.6g sodium chloride + 2.9g sodium citrate + 1.5g potassium chloride + 13.5g glucose per liter — if available use these
Children dehydrate much faster than adults — prioritize pediatric cases
avoid: Do NOT give sports drinks (Gatorade etc) as primary rehydration — too much sugar, not enough sodium
Do NOT give plain water alone to severely dehydrated person without electrolytes
Do NOT force fluids on unconscious person — aspiration risk
when_to_seek_help: No urine output for 8+ hours despite oral fluids
Person cannot hold down fluids despite small sip technique
Confusion, lethargy, or loss of consciousness
Infants with sunken fontanelle (soft spot on top of head)

infection_sepsis.title: Sepsis / Life-Threatening Infection
infection_sepsis.summary: Sepsis kills in hours. It is infection spreading to the bloodstream — every hour without treatment increases mortality significantly.
steps: Recognize sepsis: infected wound PLUS at least two of — fever above 38°C OR below 36°C, heart rate above 90/min, breathing faster than 20 breaths/min, confusion
Red streaks spreading from a wound = sepsis in progress — this is an emergency
If antibiotics are available: start immediately — amoxicillin 500mg every 8 hours, or doxycycline 100mg every 12 hours, or metronidazole 500mg every 8 hours (for abdominal/wound infections)
Aggressive fluid resuscitation: if person can drink, give 1–2 liters of ORS in first hour — sepsis causes vasodilation and relative fluid deficit
Lay person flat with legs elevated — helps maintain blood pressure
Wound source control: if the infection source is a wound, open it and drain — do NOT leave infected closed wounds in a septic patient
Monitor: check pulse, breathing, and consciousness every 15 minutes — deterioration is rapid
Keep warm: sepsis causes temperature dysregulation, cover with blanket
Do NOT give anti-fever medication (antipyretics) in sepsis shock — fever is helping fight infection
avoid: Do NOT delay antibiotics waiting for 'confirmation' — start treatment on strong suspicion
Do NOT ignore confusion in a person with a known wound — this is septic encephalopathy
Do NOT close or tightly bandage an infected wound
when_to_seek_help: Any suspected sepsis — this is a life-threatening emergency
Declining blood pressure (very weak or absent pulse at wrist), cessation of urine output, or loss of consciousness — septic shock

knots_rigging.title: Essential Survival Knots and Rigging
knots_rigging.summary: Five knots cover 95% of survival situations. Learn these and practice until you can tie them in the dark.
steps: Bowline (rescue loop that doesn't tighten): make a small loop in the rope, pass the end up through the loop, around the standing line, back down through the loop — 'the rabbit comes out of the hole, goes around the tree, and back into the hole'
Clove hitch (lash to a post): wrap rope around post, cross over itself, wrap again, tuck end under the last wrap — fast and adjustable
Square knot (join two ropes of equal thickness): left over right and under, right over left and under — remember 'left over right, right over left'
Prusik knot (ascend a rope): loop a short cord around a main rope 3 times, pass end through itself — grips under load, slides when unloaded — essential for rope climbing
Figure-8 (stopper knot / loop): make a loop, pass end around and through — strong, easily untied after loading
Improvised cordage: braid 3 strands of plant fiber (inner bark of basswood, stinging nettle stems, cattail leaves) — twist each strand clockwise while twisting the bundle counterclockwise

Lashing for structures: diagonal lashing secures poles crossing at angles; square lashing secures poles at right angles — wrap rope 8 times around both poles, then 4 times between them (frapping turns) to tighten
Trucker's hitch (mechanical advantage for tension): creates 3:1 mechanical advantage for tightening tarp, lashing loads — loop, hook through, pull — standard tarp-rigging knot
avoid: Do NOT use a square knot to join ropes under high load — it can capsize and release
Do NOT use synthetic paracord for rappelling without proper equipment
Do NOT rely on a single knot for life-safety applications without backup
when_to_seek_help: Any situation involving rope rescue of persons from heights or swift water — requires trained personnel

improvised_weapons.title: Improvised Tools for Hunting and Self-Defense
improvised_weapons.summary: In a survival situation, tools for hunting and defense increase your chances significantly. These require practice before you need them.
steps: Sling (ranged): cut two 60cm cords and attach to a pouch — practiced users can hit targets at 30+ meters with rocks; requires extensive practice

Simple bow: find a flexible, strong green wood branch (osage orange, yew, ash, black locust are ideal), 150–180cm long; tie a cord from tip to tip with moderate tension; arrows from straight dry shoots
Arrow basics: shaft must be straight (heat and bend green arrows while warm), fletching from feathers or duct tape splits, sharpened bone, flint, or metal point
Spear: sharpen end of a hardwood branch to a point, harden in fire (hold over flame until point blackens, not burns), carbon hardens the wood fibers
Slingshot: Y-shaped branch, elastic bands from inner tubes or clothing elastic, leather pouch — effective for small game hunting
Knife from rock: knap flint or obsidian (any glassy stone) by striking edge at angle with another hard rock — sharp edge comparable to surgical steel
Tripwire trap (game): tie a sapling under tension to a trigger, loop of cordage on ground below — when small animal steps in loop, sapling lifts and snares
Hunting with available tools: small game at dawn and dusk near water — most efficient use of energy; larger animals require group effort
avoid: Do NOT use improvised weapons against other humans unless in immediate life-threatening danger
Do NOT practice bowmaking with live ammunition near people
Do NOT hunt endangered or protected species regardless of situation
when_to_seek_help: Any significant injury from improvised tools — treat immediately to prevent infection

heat_exhaustion.title: Heat Exhaustion / Heat Stroke
heat_exhaustion.summary: Heat stroke is a medical emergency. The brain begins dying above 40°C core temperature. Act within minutes.
steps: Distinguish heat exhaustion from heat stroke: exhaustion = heavy sweating, weakness, cold pale clammy skin, fast weak pulse, nausea — person is still alert
Heat stroke = HOT, DRY, RED skin, very high temperature, confusion or unconsciousness — this is a life-threatening emergency
Heat exhaustion treatment: move to shade/cool area, remove excess clothing, lay down with feet elevated, give cool water to sip
Heat stroke emergency cooling (do this IMMEDIATELY): immerse in cool water if possible, or wet entire body continuously and fan vigorously — goal is to lower temperature as fast as possible
Ice or cold packs: apply to armpits, groin, neck, and wrists — major blood vessel areas — this cools blood returning to core
Fan the person continuously — evaporative cooling is extremely effective
Continue cooling until temperature drops below 39°C or the person becomes alert
Do NOT give fever-reducing medication (paracetamol, ibuprofen) for heat stroke — heat stroke is NOT mediated by the fever mechanism
Monitor: check breathing and consciousness every few minutes — can deteriorate rapidly
Prevention: work in shade, wear light-colored loose clothing, drink 500–1000ml of water per hour in hot conditions
avoid: Do NOT give cold fluids rapidly by mouth to an unconscious person — aspiration risk
Do NOT use ice-cold water total immersion — can cause shivering and paradoxically raise core temperature
Do NOT ignore confusion or behavior change in hot conditions — early heat stroke
when_to_seek_help: Any heat stroke — this is a medical emergency
Persistent confusion after cooling
Seizures associated with heat exposure
Core temperature above 41°C

poison_ingestion.title: Poisoning / Toxic Ingestion
poison_ingestion.summary: The correct treatment depends on WHAT was ingested. Inducing vomiting is wrong for many types of poisoning and can kill.
steps: Identify what was taken — name, amount, time — this determines treatment entirely
Activated charcoal: if available and person is conscious — 25–50g mixed with water, give within 1 hour of ingestion — absorbs many toxins in gut before absorption
Corrosive poisons (acids, alkalis, bleach, drain cleaner): DO NOT induce vomiting — give milk or water to dilute, do NOT give activated charcoal
Petroleum products (gasoline, turpentine): DO NOT induce vomiting — aspiration into lungs is far more dangerous than ingestion
Plant/food poisoning and most medications: if person is conscious and within 1 hour: induced vomiting MAY be appropriate — press finger to back of throat
Alcohol poisoning: lay person on their side (recovery position), monitor breathing, do NOT give coffee — keep warm
Medication overdose: if you know the medication, note the amount; do not give anything by mouth if unconscious
Recovery position for unconscious person: roll onto left side with knee drawn up, tilt head back slightly — prevents choking on vomit
Seizures from poisoning: protect the person from injury, do not restrain, time the seizure, give nothing by mouth during seizure
avoid: Do NOT induce vomiting for corrosive substances, petroleum products, or if the person is unconscious
Do NOT give 'antidotes' you read about without confirmation — many are dangerous myths
Do NOT leave poisoning victim alone — deterioration can be rapid and sudden
when_to_seek_help: Any significant poisoning — call poison control or emergency services immediately
Unconsciousness, seizures, or difficulty breathing after ingestion
Cyanide, organophosphate, or methanol poisoning — specific antidotes exist and are urgently needed

triage.title: Mass Casualty Triage — Who to Help First
triage.summary: In mass casualty events, the goal is the greatest good for the greatest number. Triage means sorting, not treating — you must move quickly.
steps: START triage system (Simple Triage And Rapid Treatment):
Step 1: Call out 'If you can walk, move to the far wall/area' — walking wounded are GREEN (minor) — move them and leave them for now
Step 2: Approach remaining casualties — check breathing
If not breathing: open airway (head tilt chin lift) — if breathing starts: RED (immediate). If still not breathing: BLACK (expectant/deceased in field conditions)
If breathing: count respiratory rate — above 30 breaths/min = RED (immediate)
Step 3: Check radial pulse at wrist — absent OR capillary refill above 2 seconds = RED (immediate)
Step 4: Mental status — cannot follow simple commands = RED (immediate)
All others who passed steps 2–4: YELLOW (delayed — can wait 30–60 minutes)
Color system: RED = treat first (can survive with immediate care), YELLOW = treat second, GREEN = treat last, BLACK = expectant (unsurvivable or already deceased)
Move through ALL casualties quickly to triage BEFORE treating anyone — spending too long on one patient costs others their lives
Reassess triage tags every 15–30 minutes — status changes
avoid: Do NOT spend more than 60 seconds per person during initial triage
Do NOT give BLACK tag impulsively — open the airway first
Do NOT let emotional response override triage logic — the goal is maximum lives saved
when_to_seek_help: As soon as scene is safe — call for emergency medical services and communicate casualty count, types of injuries, and location

communications_no_phone.title: Communication Without Electronic Devices
communications_no_phone.summary: Pre-agreed communication plans and visual/physical methods remain effective when all electronics fail.
steps: Pre-arrange rally points: agree on 2–3 meeting points BEFORE an emergency — if separated, go to primary rally point, wait 1 hour, then secondary, wait 1 hour, then tertiary
Runner system: use a reliable person as a message courier — messages should be written, specific, and signed
Code system: agree on simple codes before separation — chalk mark on wall = I was here, double mark = emergency, arrow = direction I went
Visual signals (short distance): flag signals — waving cloth = come here, cloth stretched between hands = I need help, cloth raised and lowered = understood
Mirror flash code: agree on Morse or simple flash code before separation — 1 flash = yes, 2 flashes = no, 3 flashes = emergency
Written notes and drop points: agree on specific physical locations to leave notes — inside a specific rock, behind a specific sign
Radio schedule: if you have radios, agree on specific times to transmit (e.g. 7am, 1pm, 7pm) — conserves batteries and ensures someone is listening
Acoustic signals: horn, whistle, gunshot, or drum — agree on meaning before separation; 3 signals = emergency
Night signals: flashlight code (pre-agreed flashes), fire signals (1 fire = safe, 2 fires = danger, 3 fires = emergency)
Pigeon post in extended operations: if homing pigeons are available, they reliably return to home location — ancient and proven communication method
avoid: Do NOT rely on only one communication method
Do NOT leave messages in locations accessible to hostile parties
when_to_seek_help: When communication has been established with rescue or friendly forces — pass your exact location and status immediately

vehicle_survival.title: Vehicle Emergency / Breakdown Survival
vehicle_survival.summary: A vehicle is a shelter, a signal device, and a survival resource. Use it — don't abandon it unless you must.
steps: Stay with your vehicle if stranded — it is far easier to find a vehicle than a person on foot
Signal with vehicle: run headlights intermittently (preserves battery), honk horn in 3-blast distress pattern, tie bright cloth to antenna or roof
Car battery as power source: a typical car battery stores 500–800Wh — enough to charge phones dozens of times or run 12V lights for days
Access car battery: open hood, connect devices via 12V socket in car, or connect leads directly to battery terminals (red = positive, black = negative)
Car as shelter: provides protection from wind and rain; in cold, run engine for 10 minutes per hour for heat — crack window slightly to prevent CO buildup
Vehicle fire: stop immediately, turn off engine, get everyone out, move 30 meters away — car fires can cause fuel tank explosion
If vehicle is in water: unbuckle immediately, do NOT open door until water pressure equalizes (door will open once water level outside equals inside), take deep breath, exit through window
Vehicle in conflict: park in covered position (garage, under bridge, behind buildings); remove license plates if they could identify you; cover windshield to reduce reflection
Extract from mud/sand: let some air out of tires (reduces ground pressure), place traction boards (mats, branches, clothing) under drive wheels, move slowly forward and back
Siphon fuel from tank (if available): clear plastic tubing — start siphon by mouth suction, immediately cap your end when flowing, drain to container
avoid: Do NOT run engine in an enclosed space — CO poisoning
Do NOT siphon fuel using your mouth as suction — inhaling fuel vapors is toxic
Do NOT stay in vehicle on train tracks or in a flooding riverbed
when_to_seek_help: Significant injury from accident — assess and treat immediately
Vehicle in water — every second counts
Vehicle on fire — immediate evacuation and distance

default.title: Emergency Assistance
default.summary: I'm here to help. Let me provide immediate guidance. Stay calm — panic reduces cognitive function by 30%.
steps: STOP and breathe — take 3 slow deep breaths before doing anything
Assess immediate safety: are you in danger RIGHT NOW? If yes, move first, assess second
Survival priorities in order: Air → Stop Bleeding → Temperature → Water → Food → Signaling
Take stock of resources: water, food, people, tools, location, communication devices
Establish communication if possible — let someone know your location
Rule of 3s: 3 minutes without air, 3 hours without shelter in extreme conditions, 3 days without water, 3 weeks without food
Make a simple written plan with the resources you have — externalize thinking
Help others once you yourself are stable — you cannot help others if you become a casualty
Conserve energy: rest when possible, move only when necessary, avoid panic sweating and hyperventilation
avoid: Do NOT act impulsively without assessing the situation for at least 60 seconds
Do NOT separate from your group unnecessarily
Do NOT exhaust your resources in the first hours
Do NOT assume rescue is coming — plan as if it won't, act pleasantly surprised if it does
Do NOT make irreversible decisions under panic
when_to_seek_help: Medical emergency beyond basic first aid
Immediate threat to life that cannot be managed alone
Resource depletion with no viable solution
Any situation involving children, elderly, or disabled individuals at risk

nuclear_radiation.title: Nuclear / Radiation Emergency
nuclear_radiation.summary: Nuclear events are survivable with correct immediate actions. Distance, shielding, and time are your three tools.
steps: Nuclear detonation — if you see the flash: immediately take cover behind any solid object, face down, cover exposed skin — blast wave arrives 5–30 seconds after flash
After blast wave passes: if you are able to move, go INSIDE a solid building immediately
Best shelters ranked: underground basement (best), center of large concrete building, brick building, wood building (worst but still better than outside)
GET INSIDE within 10–15 minutes of detonation — this is when fallout begins arriving
Once inside: move to center of building, away from windows and exterior walls, go to highest floor possible but below rooftop (fallout settles on roof)
Stay inside minimum 24 hours — radioactivity halves approximately every 7 hours (7-10 rule: 7 hours after blast, radiation is 1/10 of 1-hour level)
Seal building: turn off ventilation, HVAC, fans; seal gaps in windows and doors with tape, wet cloth, or plastic sheeting
Potassium iodide (KI): if available, take ONE dose per day — blocks radioactive iodine from being absorbed by thyroid gland; take BEFORE or immediately after exposure
If caught outside during fallout: cover mouth and nose, move at right angles to wind direction, get inside as fast as possible
Decontamination: remove outer clothing (removes 80% of contamination), shower thoroughly with soap — do NOT condition hair (conditions bind radioactive particles)
Radiation sickness signs (appear hours to days later): nausea, vomiting, hair loss, fatigue, skin reddening — high dose exposure
EMP effects: electronic devices, vehicles with computers (post-1980s), and power grid may be disabled — older mechanical vehicles may still work
avoid: Do NOT go outside during the first 24 hours after fallout begins
Do NOT take KI if allergic to iodine or have certain thyroid conditions without medical advice
Do NOT assume safety because you feel fine — radiation sickness is delayed
Do NOT drive through fallout areas unless absolutely necessary
when_to_seek_help: Radiation sickness symptoms: vomiting multiple times, hair loss, skin bleeding — requires medical support
When government all-clear is issued — follow official guidance on evacuation routes

gas_leak.title: Gas Leak Emergency
