gas_leak.summary: Gas leaks kill through explosion and asphyxiation. The correct response is fast and counterintuitive.
steps: If you smell gas: do NOT turn any light switches on OR off — electrical sparks ignite gas
Do NOT use any electronic device including phones inside the building
Do NOT light any flame
Open windows and doors as you evacuate — do not pause to close them
Leave the building immediately — do not collect belongings
Shut off gas main outside the building if you know where it is and it is safely accessible
Move at least 100 meters away and upwind from the building
Call emergency services ONLY after you are outside and away from the building
Do NOT re-enter until emergency services confirm the building is safe
Carbon monoxide (odorless): symptoms include headache, dizziness, nausea in enclosed spaces — evacuate immediately, fresh air is antidote
For CO poisoning: fresh air, lie down, loosen clothing — severe cases need medical oxygen
avoid: Do NOT operate any electrical switches
Do NOT use phone, radio, or any electronic device inside
Do NOT try to find the leak yourself
Do NOT re-enter building until cleared by emergency services
when_to_seek_help: Immediately after exiting — call emergency services from outside
Any person who lost consciousness from gas exposure — requires medical evaluation

snakebite.title: Snakebite Treatment
snakebite.summary: Most snakebites, even from venomous species, are survivable with correct treatment. What NOT to do is as important as what to do.
steps: Move away from the snake — do not attempt to catch or kill it
Keep the bitten person calm and still — physical activity increases venom circulation
Remove jewelry, watches, and tight clothing near the bite — swelling will be significant
Position the bitten limb BELOW heart level — slows venom transport
Clean the bite gently with soap and water if available
Mark the edge of swelling with a pen and note the time — document progression
Immobilize the limb using a splint — this genuinely reduces venom spread
Get to medical care as quickly as possible — antivenom is the only definitive treatment
Pressure immobilization bandage (for elapid snakes — cobra, mamba, krait, sea snake): wrap firmly but not tightly from bite upward toward body, immobilize entire limb — NOT for viper bites
Identify snake if safely possible — color, pattern, head shape — this helps medical staff choose correct antivenom
Dry bites (no venom injected) occur in up to 50% of venomous snakebites — still treat as venomous until confirmed otherwise
avoid: Do NOT cut and suck the bite — ineffective and causes additional tissue damage and infection
Do NOT apply tourniquet — concentrates venom and destroys tissue
Do NOT apply ice — causes additional tissue damage
Do NOT give alcohol
Do NOT apply electric shock — myth with no evidence
Do NOT apply pressure bandage for viper/rattlesnake bites — concentrates tissue-destroying venom
when_to_seek_help: Any confirmed venomous snakebite — antivenom is urgently needed
Symptoms progressing: swelling, pain, bruising spreading — envenomation confirmed
Difficulty breathing, ptosis (drooping eyelids), paralysis — neurotoxic venom, extremely urgent

allergic_reaction.title: Severe Allergic Reaction / Anaphylaxis
allergic_reaction.summary: Anaphylaxis kills in minutes through airway closure. Epinephrine is the only treatment — improvise if you must.
steps: Recognize anaphylaxis: hives + ONE OR MORE of — swollen throat/tongue, difficulty breathing, drop in blood pressure, dizziness, loss of consciousness
Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately if available: outer thigh, can inject through clothing, hold for 10 seconds
Call emergency services immediately — epinephrine effects last only 15–20 minutes, reaction can return
Lay person flat with legs elevated — do NOT sit them up if they are dizzy or unconscious
Exception: if having difficulty breathing, prop in sitting position
Give second EpiPen after 5–15 minutes if symptoms return or do not improve
Give antihistamine (diphenhydramine/Benadryl) as secondary treatment only — does NOT replace epinephrine, too slow for anaphylaxis
If no EpiPen: some evidence supports using high-dose inhaled salbutamol (asthma inhaler) for bronchospasm component — not a substitute but may help breathing temporarily
If the person stops breathing: begin CPR — anaphylaxis-related cardiac arrest often responds to CPR
Remove the trigger if possible: remove bee stinger by scraping (not squeezing)
avoid: Do NOT give antihistamine INSTEAD of epinephrine — antihistamines are too slow for anaphylaxis
Do NOT sit the person upright if they are dizzy or have low blood pressure — except if breathing is very difficult
Do NOT delay epinephrine waiting to see if symptoms improve
when_to_seek_help: IMMEDIATELY — every anaphylaxis case requires emergency medical evaluation
Even if symptoms resolve with EpiPen — biphasic reaction can occur hours later

eye_injury.title: Eye Injury Treatment
eye_injury.summary: Eye injuries require gentle handling. Wrong treatment can permanently damage vision.
steps: Chemical splash: immediately flush with clean water for minimum 15–20 minutes — hold eye open under gentle stream, flush from inner corner outward
Do NOT try to neutralize chemicals — flush with water only
Foreign object (dust, dirt): blink rapidly, flush with water, pull upper eyelid over lower and blink — often dislodges particle
Do NOT rub the eye — embeds particles and scratches cornea
Embedded object (metal, glass, wood): do NOT remove — cover both eyes with clean cloth to prevent movement, seek medical care
Blunt trauma (black eye): apply cool pack (cloth with ice) for 15–20 minutes — reduces swelling; check vision
Penetrating injury: do NOT remove object, do NOT press on the eye — cover loosely with clean cup or cone to avoid pressure, treat other injuries
Vision assessment: cover each eye separately — significant difference in vision between eyes indicates serious injury
Arc eye (from welding flash or snow blindness): painful gritty sensation in eyes appearing hours after bright light exposure — cool compresses, keep eyes closed, usually resolves in 24–48 hours
avoid: Do NOT rub any injured eye
Do NOT remove objects embedded in the eye
Do NOT use eye drops (other than preservative-free saline) without knowing what caused the injury
Do NOT patch a chemical injury — needs to be flushed continuously
when_to_seek_help: Any penetrating eye injury
Any chemical exposure to eye — requires professional irrigation
Significant vision change or loss
Embedded foreign objects

bridge_crossing.title: Crossing Water / Swift Water Survival
bridge_crossing.summary: More survival deaths occur during water crossings than almost any other activity. Assess before you commit.
steps: Never cross if: water is above knee-deep AND fast, OR you cannot see the bottom, OR the crossing point is near rapids or a waterfall downstream
Find the best crossing point: widest part of river = shallowest, slowest; look for braided channels to cross individually
Unbuckle all pack straps before entering water — you need to ditch your pack instantly if you fall
Crossing staff: use a strong stick/pole on your upstream side, leaning into current — creates a tripod with your two legs
Group crossing: link arms with partners facing each other in a line perpendicular to current — group maintains stability
Angle downstream: cross at 45° to current angled downstream — direct crossing wastes energy fighting current
If you fall in swift water: roll onto your back, feet downstream (protect your head), toes up — this is defensive swimming position
Defensive swimming: use your feet to bounce off rocks, use arms to steer toward shore, wait for calm water before trying to stand
Do NOT stand in fast current above knee depth — hydraulic forces will knock you over
Rope assisted crossing: anchor rope upstream, person crosses with rope as security — downstream person holds tension to arrest falls
avoid: Do NOT attempt to stand up in fast water if knocked over — get into defensive swimming position
Do NOT tie yourself to a rope during water crossing — if you go under you cannot release
Do NOT cross at bends in river — deepest, fastest water is on outside of bend
when_to_seek_help: After near-drowning: even if recovered, pulmonary complications can develop hours later
Hypothermia after cold water exposure

insect_stings.title: Insect Stings / Arachnid Bites
insect_stings.summary: Most insect stings are painful but not dangerous. Know which are and which aren't in your region.
steps: Bee stinger: remove by scraping with card or fingernail — do NOT squeeze with fingers (injects more venom)
Wasp/hornet sting: no stinger to remove — they can sting multiple times
Basic treatment: clean the area, apply cool compress, antihistamine if available for swelling
Scorpion sting: keep person calm, immobilize the stung limb, cool compress — most scorpion stings cause only local pain; seek help if systemic symptoms develop
Dangerous scorpion signs: muscle twitching, difficulty swallowing, difficulty breathing, excessive salivation, blurred vision — antivenom needed
Spider bites: identify if possible — most are harmless; black widow (red hourglass marking) and brown recluse (violin marking) are medically significant
Black widow: cramping pain spreading from bite, sweating, nausea — treat pain, seek antivenom
Brown recluse: expanding necrotic wound appearing over 24–72 hours — aggressive wound management and antibiotics needed
Tick removal: grasp with fine tweezers as close to skin as possible, pull straight out with steady pressure — do NOT twist, do NOT burn or apply chemicals
After tick removal: clean area, mark date, monitor for 30 days for bullseye rash (Lyme disease) or fever
Mosquito protection: cover skin dawn and dusk, use DEET repellent, use permethrin on clothing
avoid: Do NOT squeeze a bee stinger
Do NOT burn a tick or apply petroleum jelly — it does not work and stimulates regurgitation
Do NOT ignore systemic symptoms after any sting or bite
when_to_seek_help: Signs of anaphylaxis after any sting — immediately
Scorpion sting with systemic symptoms
Expanding wound or necrosis at bite site
Fever and bullseye rash after tick bite — Lyme disease

childrens_emergency.title: Pediatric Emergency — Infants and Children
childrens_emergency.summary: Children are not small adults — dosing, techniques, and thresholds differ significantly. When in doubt, be more conservative.
steps: Infant CPR (under 1 year): 2 fingers on center of chest, push down 4cm at 100–120/min, 30 compressions to 2 breaths (mouth covers both nose and mouth)
Child CPR (1–8 years): one hand heel on chest, push down 5cm at 100–120/min, 30:2 ratio
Infant choking (conscious): 5 back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand, then 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers — alternate until object dislodges
Child choking (over 1 year): Heimlich maneuver — stand behind, 2 fingers above navel, upward thrusts
Fever in infants under 3 months: any temperature above 38°C is a medical emergency — seek help immediately
Fever 3 months – 2 years: above 39°C warrants medical evaluation; above 40°C is urgent
Febrile seizure: lay child on side, do NOT restrain, do NOT put anything in mouth, time the seizure — usually lasts under 5 minutes and is not life-threatening though terrifying
Dehydration in children: look for dry diapers (over 6 hours), sunken eyes, no tears when crying, sunken fontanelle in infants — rehydrate with ORS immediately
Medication dosing: always weight-based in children — paracetamol 15mg per kilogram, ibuprofen 10mg per kilogram every 6–8 hours (over 6 months only)
avoid: Do NOT give aspirin to children under 16
Do NOT give ibuprofen to infants under 6 months
Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) on infants under 1 year
Do NOT restrain a child having a febrile seizure
when_to_seek_help: Any fever in infant under 3 months
Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes
Child with severe dehydration
Child who remains unconscious or doesn't return to normal after a seizure

communication_security.title: Communication Security in Conflict Zones
communication_security.summary: In conflict zones, your communications may be monitored. Operational security (OPSEC) can be the difference between life and death.
steps: Assume all electronic communications are monitored in conflict zones — act accordingly
Use Signal app if available — end-to-end encrypted, best available consumer tool; enable disappearing messages
Phone discipline: remove SIM card and battery when not in use — IMSI catchers can track you even without a call
Code words: pre-agree simple code words for dangerous/safe locations, need help, all clear — communicated as normal conversation
Avoid patterns: vary your routes, times, communication frequency — patterns are how intelligence services identify you
Written message security: if writing sensitive information, write on temporary medium (non-porous surface with erasable marker), photograph if needed, erase
Dead drop communication: pre-agree a physical location to leave messages — both parties can access location at different times without meeting
Face-to-face security: only discuss sensitive matters in open outdoor locations where observation and listening devices are impractical
Device security: enable full device encryption, use strong PIN (not fingerprint — can be compelled), enable remote wipe
Destroy sensitive information before capture: hard drive: multiple passes of overwrite or physical destruction; paper: burn or eat if necessary
avoid: Do NOT transmit location, plans, or personnel information on unsecured channels
Do NOT use the same communication method repeatedly if you suspect surveillance
Do NOT discuss sensitive operations on any electronic device in a building
when_to_seek_help: If you believe you are under active surveillance — change routes, locations, and communication methods immediately

psychological_first_aid.title: Psychological First Aid — Helping Trauma Survivors
psychological_first_aid.summary: After traumatic events, psychological support is as critical as physical. The right approach prevents lasting psychological injury.
steps: The 5 principles of Psychological First Aid: Safety, Calm, Connectedness, Self-Efficacy, Hope
Approach: introduce yourself calmly, ask what they need — do not assume
Ensure physical safety and basic needs first — water, warmth, shelter before psychological support
Listen without judgment: do NOT tell someone they shouldn't feel a certain way — validate feelings
Do NOT force people to talk about what happened — this is NOT debriefing and forcing recall can worsen trauma
Active listening: make eye contact (culturally appropriate), use open body language, reflect back what you hear
Help the person connect with their support network: family, friends, community — social connection is the strongest buffer against PTSD
Provide accurate information: uncertainty amplifies anxiety — give honest, simple information about current situation
Identify those at high risk: those who lost family members, those who are alone, children, those with prior trauma or mental illness
Normalize reactions: 'What you are feeling is a normal response to an abnormal situation' — this is literally true and very powerful
Give the person something to DO — a simple task — restores sense of agency and control
Children: return to routine as quickly as possible, allow regression in behavior without punishment, provide honest simple explanations
avoid: Do NOT force people to relive trauma by asking for detailed accounts
Do NOT make promises you cannot keep about outcomes
Do NOT express excessive pity — it disempowers
Do NOT isolate people — social connection is healing
when_to_seek_help: Person is unable to function at all after 48 hours
Suicidal statements or self-harm
Psychosis: person losing touch with reality
Children who stop speaking or eating

electricity_wiring.title: Emergency Electrical Wiring and Power Distribution
electricity_wiring.summary: Safe electrical wiring is achievable with basic knowledge. Wrong connections cause fires and deaths — understand before you touch.
steps: Always disconnect power source before working on any wiring
Wire identification: in most countries — brown/black = live (hot), blue/white = neutral, green/yellow = earth (ground)
Basic circuit: power source → switch → load (light/device) → back to source — all connections must be solid and insulated
Wire connections: strip 1cm of insulation, twist conductors tightly together, cover completely with electrical tape or wire nut
Fuse calculation: fuse rating should be 80% of maximum current for the circuit — a 10 amp circuit needs a 10 amp fuse, NOT a 20 amp fuse
Improvised fuse: if you must, a short length of thin copper wire will melt before thicker wire — this is a last resort, not standard practice
Extension cord safety: never daisy-chain multiple extension cords, never cover with carpet or run under doors, never exceed the cord's rated amperage
Ground fault: if someone is being shocked by a device, the device has a fault — disconnect at source, do not touch device or person until power is off
12V DC systems (solar/battery): much safer than AC — bare wire contact causes sparks and burns but rarely death; same wiring principles apply
Solar charge controller wiring sequence: 1. Connect battery first, 2. Connect solar panel, 3. Connect load — always in this order, always reverse to disconnect
avoid: Do NOT work on live wires
Do NOT use a larger fuse than specified — this defeats the purpose of a fuse
Do NOT use tape as the only insulation in permanent installations
Do NOT mix AC and DC wiring in the same conduit
when_to_seek_help: Any electrical fire — disconnect power source, use CO2 or dry powder extinguisher, never water on electrical fire
Anyone shocked by AC current — internal burns are invisible

long_term_survival.title: Long-Term / Extended Survival Planning
long_term_survival.summary: Extended survival beyond 72 hours requires systems, not just supplies. Community organization is your greatest multiplier.
steps: Community formation: immediately identify skills in your group — medical, mechanical, agricultural, security — assign roles based on ability
Security rotation: establish watch shifts (2 hours per person, 4 people minimum for 24-hour coverage without exhaustion)
Water system: establish a reliable, repeatable daily water collection and purification routine — assign dedicated responsibility
Food system: caloric accounting — know what you have, how long it lasts, and when to start supplementing with foraging and hunting
Agricultural basics: any seeds can be planted — focus on fast-growing, calorie-dense crops: root vegetables (potatoes, sweet potatoes), legumes (beans), leafy greens
Soil amendment: human and animal waste is excellent fertilizer after composting for minimum 3 months (pathogens killed by heat and time)
Seed saving: save seeds from non-hybrid produce — allow some plants to bolt and seed, collect and dry seeds for next planting
Medical inventory: establish a community medical kit, train at least 2 people in each group in first aid, keep a written record of any medical conditions and medications
Skill transfer: every person with a critical skill should teach at least one other person — redundancy is survival
Record keeping: write down everything — resource counts, plant growth, medical events, weather patterns — paper documentation outlasts memory
Energy planning: establish renewable energy sources (solar, wind, hydro) as permanent infrastructure, not emergency stopgap
avoid: Do NOT hoard resources without contributing to community — creates conflict that is more dangerous than resource scarcity
Do NOT neglect mental health and social cohesion — community breakdown kills more people than physical threats in long-term survival
Do NOT plant everything at once — stagger plantings for continuous harvest
when_to_seek_help: When organized government or aid services become accessible — integrate with larger support systems
Medical conditions exceeding your treatment capacity

animal_threats.title: Large Predator and Animal Threat Response
animal_threats.summary: Wild animal attacks are rare but follow predictable patterns. The correct response is specific to the species.
steps: Bear (black bear): be aggressive — make yourself big, make noise, do NOT run, fight back if attacked — black bears respond to dominance
Bear (brown/grizzly): if attacked while surprised, play dead — face down, hands protecting neck, legs spread — maintain position until bear leaves; if predatory attack (stalking), fight back
Cougar/mountain lion: make yourself large, maintain eye contact, back away slowly — do NOT run (triggers chase instinct), fight back if attacked
Wolf pack: same as cougar — appear large, maintain eye contact, do NOT run, back away slowly; wolves respond to pack-predator instinct
Crocodile on land: run in a straight line — crocodiles are fast in short bursts but tire quickly; zigzagging is a myth that slows you down
Crocodile in water: if grabbed, gouge eyes and strike nose — these are pressure-sensitive areas; do NOT try to pry jaws open
Shark: if attacked, punch snout or gouge eyes — fight back; playing dead does not work with sharks
Hippo: if encountered on land, immediately climb a tree or get behind a solid obstacle — hippos are extremely territorial and fast; do NOT run in open ground
Elephant: bluff charge (ears out, noise) vs real charge (ears back, silent, trunk tucked) — bluff: stand firm and make noise; real charge: take solid cover immediately
General: avoid dawn/dusk near water in predator country, make noise while moving through dense vegetation, never approach any wild animal regardless of apparent tameness
avoid: Do NOT run from any large predator — triggers chase instinct
Do NOT make eye contact with primates — seen as threat display
Do NOT feed or approach any wild animal
Do NOT get between a mother and offspring
when_to_seek_help: Any significant animal bite or scratch — infection and rabies risk
Any crocodile or large predator attack — internal injuries common

document_preservation.title: Protecting Critical Documents
document_preservation.summary: In conflict zones and disasters, identity documents can mean the difference between crossing a border and being detained.
steps: Priority documents: passport, national ID, birth certificate, marriage certificate, medical records, property documents, financial records
Waterproofing: place in sealed zip-lock bag, then inside a second bag — double seal; or laminate if equipment available
Digital copies: photograph every document with phone camera — upload to cloud storage accessible from any device; email copies to yourself and a trusted contact
Distribute copies: keep one physical copy on your person, one in your bag, leave one with a trusted person in a different location
Memory: memorize your passport number, date of birth, and government ID number — information survives even if documents don't
Medical information card: write on waterproof paper or laminate — blood type, medications, allergies, medical conditions, emergency contacts
Cash: keep small amounts of local and international currency (USD/EUR) in multiple locations on your body — not all in one wallet
Important contacts: write key phone numbers on your body or on waterproof paper — phone memory is useless if device is destroyed
avoid: Do NOT keep all documents and cash in one location
Do NOT store digital copies only on the device you carry — cloud backup essential
when_to_seek_help: Document loss at border or checkpoint — contact your country's embassy or consulate immediately

spinal_injury.title: Possible Spinal Injury
spinal_injury.summary: Numbness, paralysis, or severe back/neck pain after trauma may indicate spinal cord injury. Movement can worsen permanent damage.
steps: DO NOT move unless there is immediate life-threatening danger.
Keep your head, neck, and back completely still.
If another person is helping, stabilize your head using both hands.
Check breathing carefully without twisting the neck.
Call emergency medical services immediately.
Keep warm using blankets or clothing to reduce shock risk.
Monitor consciousness and breathing continuously.
avoid: Do not stand or walk.
Do not twist the neck or spine.
Do not attempt stretching or massage.
Do not lift the person unnecessarily.
when_to_seek_help: Immediately after numbness or paralysis.
If tingling spreads to arms or legs.
If loss of bladder or bowel control occurs.
If breathing becomes difficult.
After any serious fall with neck or back pain.

plant_identification.title: Edible and Toxic Plant Identification
plant_identification.summary: Eat the wrong plant and you die. Universal Edibility Test is your protocol when unsure. When completely unsure, do not eat.
steps: Universal Edibility Test (takes 8+ hours — do only if desperate): 1. Separate plant into components (leaves, stem, root, fruit) and test one part at a time
2. Check for contact reaction: crush and rub on inner forearm, wait 15 minutes for rash/burning/numbness
3. Mouth test: touch to outer lip, wait 3 minutes; touch to tongue, wait 3 minutes; chew a small amount, wait 15 minutes without swallowing; swallow a small amount, wait 8 hours
4. If no reaction after 8 hours: larger amount is likely safe — cook before eating if possible
Generally SAFE indicators: milky or clear (not white/milky like latex), no bitter/soapy/burning taste, no almond scent (cyanide), leaves in alternate not opposite arrangement
Safe plant families you can often recognize: grasses (seeds are almost always safe cooked), conifers (pine needles for tea, inner bark), clovers, dandelions

DANGEROUS plant families: Apiaceae/carrot family (contains some deadly species — hemlock looks like wild carrot), Solanaceae (nightshade family — many toxic), all mushrooms require expert identification
Safe berries: tend to be blue, black, or red aggregated berries (raspberries, blackberries pattern). AVOID white or yellow berries — most are toxic
Definitive rule: if you don't know it, don't eat it — 3 weeks without food won't kill you; the wrong mushroom will
avoid: Do NOT eat any mushroom without absolute expert identification — many deadly species look identical to edible ones
Do NOT eat plants with milky sap — latex-producing plants are usually toxic
Do NOT eat plants with umbel (umbrella-shaped) flowers unless positively identified as safe — this family contains water hemlock and poison hemlock
Do NOT assume birds eating a berry means it is safe for humans — birds tolerate many toxins humans cannot
when_to_seek_help: Any suspected plant poisoning — activated charcoal if available, emergency medical care
Mushroom poisoning: symptoms can be delayed 6–24 hours — liver failure is the danger

pregnancy_complications.title: Pregnancy Complications
pregnancy_complications.summary: Pregnancy emergencies kill quickly. Know the warning signs and act immediately.
steps: Heavy vaginal bleeding during pregnancy: lay the woman down, elevate legs, give IV fluids if available, get to medical care immediately — placenta praevia or abruption
Eclampsia (seizure in pregnant woman): position on left side (reduces pressure on major vein), protect from injury, do NOT restrain, get to hospital
Preeclampsia warning signs: severe headache, visual disturbances, swelling of hands/face, upper right abdominal pain, high blood pressure — precedes eclampsia
First trimester severe pain + bleeding: could be ectopic pregnancy (embryo in fallopian tube) — can rupture and cause fatal internal bleeding; urgent surgical emergency
Preterm labor (before 37 weeks): contractions before 37 weeks — if birth is imminent, prepare to receive baby; if not, try to get to medical facility

Cord prolapse (cord comes before baby): if cord is visible, do NOT push it back — have mother assume knee-chest position (on knees, chest to floor) to reduce cord compression, get to emergency care immediately

Placenta delivered with excessive bleeding after birth: massage uterine fundus (feel for firm round mass below navel and firmly massage), breastfeeding stimulates natural oxytocin which contracts uterus
Monitor: normal blood loss after delivery is up to 500ml; more than 500ml is postpartum hemorrhage
avoid: Do NOT let a woman with suspected eclampsia remain alone
Do NOT ignore first trimester pain — ectopic pregnancy rupture is rapidly fatal
Do NOT restrict a pregnant woman's fluid intake during labor
when_to_seek_help: Any seizure in a pregnant woman
Heavy bleeding during any stage of pregnancy
Cord prolapse — extreme urgency
Signs of preeclampsia — high urgency
